title: Spot the ISS
description: Use a satellite-tracking app on your smartphone and a basic telescope to watch the International Space Station pass overhead and log the time in your mission logbook.

dialogue.text: The ISS crosses the sky a few times each week. Let's plan a safe viewing and log the pass like a flight controller.

options.text: Help me pick the right pass.
I'll set up my observing spot.

dialogue.text: Open a satellite-tracking app such as Heavens-Above, allow location access, and pick a pass above 30° elevation. Note the time and direction in your mission logbook so you know where to aim.

options.text: The app shows a bright pass soon.
Logged the time and azimuth in my book

dialogue.text: Pick an open area away from traffic and bright lights. Level the tripod, cap the telescope until you're facing away from the Sun, and clear any trip hazards before it gets dark.

options.text: Scope is balanced and pointed along the path.
Clouds rolled in; I need a later pass.

dialogue.text: Start a two-minute countdown, then sweep ahead of the path so the ISS glides into view. Track gently with the slow-motion controls and note its brightness and direction of travel.

options.text: I watched with binoculars; logging the time.

dialogue.text: In your mission logbook, note the start time, maximum elevation, and when the ISS entered Earth's shadow. Those details make the next pass easier to predict.

options.text: Entry recorded with time and brightness
I'll jot it down after I pack up.

dialogue.text: Awesome! It's inspiring to see humans living in orbit. Cap the telescope, close the app, pack up your gear, and watch your footing in the dark.
